segments.s275-g1: نخستین is fronted and here functions adverbially/attributively as 'first'. فریبرز is the proper name (the subject). نیو دلیر is a postpositive epithet attached to the name (نام + صفت), with نیو as a regional/poetic form meaning 'new' or part of the epithet and دلیر 'brave'. The whole phrase names and qualifies the warrior who acts in the next segment.
segments.s275-g2: ز + لشکر + برون is a prepositional phrase 'out from the host/army' with ز indicating origin and برون marking motion outward. تاخت is the past verb 'rushed/galloped' describing the action. بر سان شیر is a simile 'like a lion' (بر = 'on/like' in simile construction; سان = 'such/as'). Word order: prepositional origin → verb → simile phrase.
segments.s275-g3: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near'; it introduces the place approached. کلباد ویسه دمان here are names in apposition (Kalbad and Viseh‑Daman), with دمان appearing as part of the compound name rather than a literal 'hem' in this context.
segments.s275-g4: بیامد is simple past 'he came/approached'. به + (noun phrase) here functions adverbially 'with / having' so that به زه برنهاده کمان is an adjunct: 'with the bow placed on the string' (برنهاده is a past participle 'placed/presented' modifying کمان). The phrase describes his state as he approached.
segments.s275-g5: همی گشت uses همی to give a continuous/habitual sense 'kept turning / was turning'. گشت is past/imperfective 'turned/moved about'. و links clauses. تیرش نیامد is 'his arrow did not come' meaning the arrow did not fly or would not be sent. چو خواست is a temporal subordinate 'when he wished/tried' (خواست is past tense 'he wanted/tried').
segments.s275-g6: کشید is the past of کشیدن here meaning 'drew / pulled out' (he drew an arrow). آن پرنداور is the direct object: 'that bird‑catcher' — a poetic name for an arrow that catches birds. از دست راست is a source/instrument phrase 'from (his) right hand' (از marking origin and راست 'right'). Word order is verb → object → origin phrase.
segments.s275-g7: برآورد is a past verb 'raised/drew up' (here used with a sword, i.e., he lifted or brought up his weapon) followed by و زد 'and struck' (زد = past 'struck'). تیغ is the instrument 'sword' and بر گردنش is the direction/target 'upon his neck' (بر = onto/against, گردنش = 'his neck'). The sequence describes a single contiguous action: he raised and then struck at the neck.
segments.s275-g8: به دو نیم شد is a passive/resultative construction 'was made into two halves' (به + مقدار 'two halves' + شد 'became/was made'). تا marks extent 'as far as / up to' and introduces کمرگه تنش 'the small of his back / waist of his body' (کمرگه = waist, تنش = 'his body'). Together: his body was cleft into two up to the waist.
segments.s275-g9: فرود آمد is a light verb construction meaning 'he alighted / dismounted' (فرود = descent + آمد = came). از اسب is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the horse'. و 'and' joins the next clause. بگشاد is a literary past form (ب‑ prefix + گشاد, here functioning as a verb 'opened/loosened'). بند is the direct object 'strap' that the verb governs; the object marker را is omitted here and appears later.
segments.s275-g10: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'. فتراک خویش is 'his saddle‑cloth' (فتراک = saddle‑cloth; خویش written here as two items: خوی + ش) with the third‑person possessive suffix ش attached. آن کیانی کمند is a noun phrase 'that Kayanian (royal) lasso' placed after the prepositional phrase and serving as the thing being freed; word order places the freed object after the source phrase.
segments.s275-g11: ببست is a past transitive verb 'he bound/tied' (classical narrative form). از بر باره is a prepositional phrase meaning 'onto / upon the pack/burden' (بر باره = the baggage or load). کلباد را is the definite direct object (personal name KALBAD + را marking the object) placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s275-g12: گشاد is here a past/transitive use 'he loosened/unfastened' (literary verb form). از برش is a source phrase 'from/ off its ...' where ش is a 3rd‑person possessive clitic attached (i.e. 'from off it/its ...'). بند پولاد را is the direct object 'the steel band' (پولاد = steel) with را marking it; the clause therefore says he loosened the steel band from its fastening.
segments.s275-g13: به بالا برآمد is a verb phrase 'he rose / climbed up' (به + بالا = upward, برآمد = rose/emerged). به پیروز نام is a small clause meaning 'uttering the name Pirooz' or 'calling the name "Pirooz"' — به + name + نام is a classical way to indicate the name being invoked or cried.
segments.s275-g14: خروشی is the noun 'a shout/roar' serving as the object; برآورد is the verb 'he produced/raised' (classical برآوردن = to bring forth, utter). و 'and' links the next action. بگذارد گام literally 'he placed/put a step' (بگذارد used in narrative as 'he set/advanced'); گام is the noun 'step' (the thing placed).
segments.s275-g15: که introduces an optative/wish clause here ('that / may'). سالار ما is 'our commander' (noun + possessive). باد is the optative particle 'may (he be)' — a fossilized subjunctive used for blessings/wishes. پیروزگر is the predicate noun 'victor/conqueror' following the optative.
segments.s275-g16: همه دشمن is 'all (the) enemies' (subject/complement to 'پیروزگر' — i.e. 'conqueror of all enemies'). شاه خسته‌جگر follows as a genitival/qualifying phrase 'of the king, (who is) heart‑weary' or as a direct address 'O weary‑hearted king'; classical Persian often allows such compact apposition without an explicit ezāfe.

sentences.s275-s1: First, Fereyburz Niu the Brave dashed out from the host like a lion.
sentences.s275-s2: He came up near Kalbad Viseh‑Daman, his bow set on the string.
sentences.s275-s3: He kept turning, and when he tried his arrow would not come; he drew that bird‑catching (arrow) from his right hand.
sentences.s275-s4: He raised and struck with his sword at his neck; his body was split in two as far as the waist.
sentences.s275-s5: He dismounted from his horse and loosened the strap, freeing from his saddle-cloth that Kayanian lasso.
sentences.s275-s6: He bound Kalbad to the pack, and unfastened the steel band from its fastening.
sentences.s275-s7: He climbed up; calling the name 'Pirooz', he raised a shout and set his foot forward.
sentences.s275-s8: May our commander be triumphant, the conqueror of all the king's enemies, O heart‑wearied king.
